# Executive Summary {-}

## Key Points {-}

- This working paper summarises analyses completed in the
  third year (2025/26) of the ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn
  Dynamics project.

- We used computer simulations to test whether a proposed
  harvest rule could safely re-open the WCVI herring
  fishery. The rule works by estimating how many herring
  are present, ensuring enough are left to spawn (a
  "minimum escapement" level), and only allowing fishing
  on the surplus above that level.

- We tested this rule under three different views of how
  herring populations work, ranging from the standard DFO
  assessment model to models that include predation by
  Humpback Whales, sea lions, seals, and Pacific Hake.
  Including predation reduces the expected herring
  population size substantially.

- **When predation is not included** (current DFO approach),
  the harvest rule performs well: the population stays
  above conservation thresholds 96\% of the time, and
  average catches are approximately 1,600 t per year.

- **When predation is included**, the herring population
  is smaller and the fishery would be closed most of the
  time because the population is already near or below
  the minimum escapement level.

- **The method used to divide the catch among Stat Areas
  (23, 24, and 25) makes very little difference** to
  outcomes under any of the models tested.

- The key finding is that whether the fishery can re-open
  depends primarily on assumptions about predation, not
  on how the catch is distributed among areas. Resolving
  uncertainty about predation mortality is the most
  important next step for WCVI herring management.

## Project Summary {-}

This project develops a Nuu-chah-nulth approach to
ecosystem-based fisheries management (EBFM) by integrating
life-history traits (e.g., age, size, fecundity) and
spawning dynamics (e.g., location, timing) in herring
population models for the West Coast of Vancouver Island
(WCVI). These models represent key assumptions about how
herring populations respond to changing ecosystem conditions
and fisheries on a finer spatial scale than is considered in
current assessment models for WCVI Pacific Herring.

In 2024/25 (Phase 2), population dynamics models were
expanded to include spatial stock structure (three stocks
corresponding to Stat Areas 23, 24, and 25), predation
mortality from major herring predators, and age-dependent
egg production.

This paper summarises analyses conducted in the third year
(2025/26). Building on the three-stock predation model
developed in Phase 2, we developed a closed-loop simulation
framework (management strategy evaluation) to test a
proposed WCVI herring re-opening procedure under three
operating models of increasing ecological complexity:
(i) the *SISCAH-DDM* density-dependent mortality model,
(ii) the *SISCAH-1S-Pred* single-stock model with
explicit predation, and (iii) the *SISCAH-3S-Pred*
three-stock model with predation. The *SISCAH-3S-Pred*
model serves as a robustness test, representing a scenario
where the true population is spatially structured while the
management procedure treats WCVI herring as a single stock.
Results demonstrate how assumptions about predation and
spatial structure affect the expected performance of harvest
control rules and spatial catch allocation methods.
